title: SEGMENT 14 — Pasca's assembloids: fusing organoids into circuits

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Pasca's assembloids: fusing organoids into circuits
Let's pivot now to one of the most exciting recent developments in the field, which has come from the lab of Sergiu Pasca at Stanford.
Pasca is a physician-scientist who works on neurodevelopmental disorders, and his lab has developed an extension of cerebral organoid technology called assembloids.
Here's the idea.
The brain isn't one tissue; it's many tissues connected together in a specific architecture.
The cortex is connected to the thalamus, which is connected to brainstem, which is connected to spinal cord, which is connected to muscle.
The cortex sends axons to the striatum to drive movement.
The retina sends axons to the lateral geniculate nucleus, which sends axons to visual cortex.
Most brain function depends on these long-range connections between specialized regions.
A standard cerebral organoid contains a mixture of regions, but the architecture is messy and the connections between regions are not particularly well-defined.
To study a specific circuit, you'd want a more controlled system.
Pasca's insight was: grow two organoids separately, then fuse them.
Take a cortical organoid.
Take a striatal organoid.
Bring them into physical contact in a dish.
Let them fuse.
The neurons in the cortical organoid will extend axons that grow across the boundary into the striatal organoid.
The neurons in the striatal organoid will form connections back.
After a few weeks of co-culture, you have a single fused organoid — an assembloid — that contains both cortical and striatal regions with axonal projections between them.
You've recapitulated, in primitive form, a real anatomical connection from the brain.
The first major assembloid paper from the Pasca group was published in 2017, in Nature, and showed exactly this for the cortex and a ventral forebrain region.

They demonstrated that GABAergic interneurons — inhibitory neurons that are born in the ventral forebrain and migrate during development to populate the cortex — would, in the assembloid, migrate from the ventral region into the cortical region, just as they do in a real embryo.
They watched cells migrate, in real time, between organoids — a developmental process that takes weeks of human gestation, recapitulated in a dish in a few days.
This was a stunning demonstration.
Two organoids that fuse — Pasca puts cortical plus striatal together and watches neurons migrate from one into the other.

It showed that organoid technology could be used to study not just single-region biology but inter-regional biology — the migration, connectivity, and circuit-formation events that depend on multiple brain regions interacting.
Since then, the Pasca lab and others have built increasingly elaborate assembloids.
Cortex-thalamus assembloids, which model thalamocortical projections (the major pathway for sensory information into the cortex).
Cortex-spinal cord-muscle assembloids, which model the corticospinal motor system, complete with muscle contractions in response to cortical neuron activity.
Multi-region forebrain assembloids that include cortex, striatum, and thalamus together.
Each assembloid type lets researchers ask circuit-level questions in ways that single organoids cannot.
What are these systems useful for?
Quite a lot.
Disease modeling.

Many neurodevelopmental disorders — autism, schizophrenia, Timothy syndrome (a calcium channelopathy Pasca has worked on extensively) — are believed to involve disrupted circuit formation between specific brain regions.
By making assembloids from patient iPSCs, you can ask whether the connection between region A and region B is abnormal in this patient, and what that means for circuit function.
Pasca's group has done compelling work on Timothy syndrome assembloids, showing specific defects in cortical interneuron migration that recapitulate aspects of the disease.
Drug screening.
If you can establish a circuit-level assay in an assembloid — say, the proportion of cortical interneurons that migrate appropriately — you can screen drugs that rescue that phenotype.
This is a more functionally relevant assay than just looking at single-cell phenotypes.
Developmental biology.
Assembloids let researchers replay specific aspects of human brain development that were previously inaccessible.
When does this projection form?
What signals guide it?
What happens if you disrupt this signal?
These questions can be answered in assembloids in ways they could never be answered in living humans.
There's a beautiful conceptual point here.
Real brain function depends not on cells, but on circuits — patterns of connectivity between cells in different regions.
Single-region organoids capture cell biology.
Assembloids start to capture circuit biology.
That's a qualitatively different kind of model.
And it opens up the possibility, in the future, of modeling much more sophisticated aspects of human neural function in dishes than was conceivable a decade ago.
It's also one of the developments that intensifies the ethical questions we just discussed.
A multi-region assembloid with cortical, thalamic, and brainstem components is more sophisticated than a single-region organoid.
It's not anywhere near a whole brain, but it's closer than what came before.
The moral-status question gets sharper as the complexity increases.
The field will have to keep grappling with this as assembloid technology matures.
